topic: Standard Separation Techniques for Mixtures (Part I)

subUnits.title: 5.1 Classification of Substances and Basic Separation Methods
notes: Substances can be classified as elements, compounds, or mixtures:
• Elements: Pure substances made of one type of atom (e.g., iron, oxygen, gold)
• Compounds: Pure substances made of two or more elements chemically combined (e.g., water H₂O, salt NaCl)
• Mixtures: Two or more substances physically combined (e.g., air, sand and water)
Mixtures can be separated using physical methods based on differences in physical properties:
1. Filtration: Separates insoluble solids from liquids using filter paper
- Example: Separating sand from water
- The solid remains on the filter paper (residue), liquid passes through (filtrate)
2. Evaporation: Separates soluble solids from liquids by heating
- Example: Obtaining salt from salt water
- The liquid evaporates, leaving the solid behind
3. Decantation: Pouring off liquid carefully to leave sediment behind
- Example: Separating water from settled sand
- Used when solid particles are heavy and settle quickly
4. Floatation: Separating materials based on density differences
- Example: Separating sawdust from sand (sawdust floats, sand sinks)
5. Froth Floatation: Used in mining to separate valuable minerals from ore
- Air bubbles carry hydrophobic particles to the surface
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing filtration setup: funnel with filter paper, beaker collecting filtrate, residue on filter paper
Illustration of evaporation process: evaporating dish on tripod stand with Bunsen burner, salt crystals forming
Diagram of decantation: beaker with settled sediment, liquid being poured off carefully
Floatation demonstration: container showing sawdust floating on water while sand sinks to bottom

quiz.q: Which separation technique is used to separate an insoluble solid from a liquid?
quiz.explanation: Filtration separates insoluble solids from liquids using filter paper. The solid remains as residue while the liquid passes through as filtrate.

quiz.q: In filtration, the liquid that passes through the filter paper is called:
quiz.explanation: The filtrate is the liquid that passes through the filter paper, while the solid left behind is called the residue.

quiz.q: Evaporation is used to separate:
opts: A soluble solid from a liquid
An insoluble solid from a liquid
quiz.explanation: Evaporation separates a soluble solid (like salt) from a liquid (like water) by heating to evaporate the liquid, leaving the solid behind.

quiz.q: Decantation involves:
opts: Pouring off liquid carefully to leave sediment
quiz.explanation: Decantation is the careful pouring of liquid from a container, leaving the settled solid sediment behind.

quiz.q: Floatation separates substances based on:
quiz.explanation: Floatation separates materials based on density differences - lighter materials float while heavier ones sink.

quiz.q: Which method would you use to separate sand from water?
quiz.explanation: Sand is insoluble in water, so filtration or decantation can be used to separate them.

quiz.q: To obtain salt from seawater, the best method is:
quiz.explanation: Salt is soluble in water, so evaporation is used to remove the water and leave the salt crystals behind.

quiz.q: A mixture is different from a compound because:
opts: Mixtures can be separated by physical methods
quiz.explanation: Mixtures can be separated by physical methods (filtration, evaporation, etc.) while compounds require chemical methods to separate.

quiz.q: Froth floatation is commonly used in:
quiz.explanation: Froth floatation is used in mining to separate valuable minerals from ore by using air bubbles to carry hydrophobic particles to the surface.

quiz.q: Which statement about elements is TRUE?
opts: Elements can be broken down into simpler substances
Elements are made of two or more types of atoms
Elements are pure substances made of one type of atom
Elements can be separated by filtration
quiz.explanation: Elements are pure substances consisting of only one type of atom and cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means.

subUnits.title: 5.2 Crystallization and Fractional Crystallization
notes: Crystallization is a separation technique used to obtain pure solid compounds from solutions:
Principle: Based on the fact that solubility of most solids increases with temperature
Process of Crystallization:
1. Dissolve the impure solid in minimum amount of hot solvent
2. Filter the hot solution to remove insoluble impurities
3. Allow the solution to cool slowly
4. Pure crystals form as the solution cools (solubility decreases)
5. Filter to collect the crystals
6. Dry the crystals between filter papers
Examples:
• Obtaining pure salt from impure salt
• Purifying sugar from sugar solution
• Obtaining copper(II) sulfate crystals from solution
Fractional Crystallization:
• Used to separate two or more soluble solids with different solubilities
• The solid with lower solubility crystallizes out first
• Example: Separating a mixture of sodium chloride and potassium nitrate
• KNO₃ has higher solubility than NaCl at high temperatures
• When hot solution cools, KNO₃ crystallizes first, leaving NaCl in solution
Applications:
• Purification of chemicals in laboratories
• Production of pure salts in industries
• Separation of rare earth metals
• Sugar refining industry
diagramDescriptions: Crystallization setup: beaker with hot solution on tripod stand, crystals forming as solution cools
Step-by-step crystallization process diagram showing: dissolving → filtering → cooling → crystal formation → filtration
Fractional crystallization diagram showing two different crystal types forming at different temperatures
Solubility curve graph showing how solubility changes with temperature for different salts
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Crystallization fractional crystallization chemistry SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Crystallization is used to separate:
opts: A pure solid from a solution
An insoluble solid from a liquid
quiz.explanation: Crystallization separates a pure solid from a solution by forming crystals as the solution cools and solubility decreases.

quiz.q: The principle behind crystallization is that:
opts: Solubility of most solids increases with temperature
quiz.explanation: Most solids are more soluble in hot solvents than in cold solvents. When a hot saturated solution cools, excess solute crystallizes out.

quiz.q: In crystallization, the solution should be:
quiz.explanation: The impure solid is dissolved in minimum amount of hot solvent to create a concentrated hot solution, which yields maximum crystals upon cooling.

quiz.q: Fractional crystallization is used to separate:
opts: Two or more soluble solids with different solubilities
quiz.explanation: Fractional crystallization separates two or more soluble solids based on their different solubilities in the same solvent.

quiz.q: In fractional crystallization, which solid crystallizes first?
quiz.explanation: The solid with lower solubility crystallizes out first when the solution cools, while the more soluble solid remains in solution.

quiz.q: To separate a mixture of NaCl and KNO₃ by fractional crystallization:
quiz.explanation: KNO₃ has much higher solubility than NaCl at high temperatures but lower solubility at low temperatures, so it crystallizes first upon cooling.

quiz.q: After crystallization, crystals are dried by:
quiz.explanation: Crystals are gently dried by placing them between filter papers to absorb moisture without decomposing the crystals.

quiz.q: An application of crystallization is:
quiz.explanation: Crystallization is used in the sugar industry to purify sugar from sugar cane juice by forming pure sugar crystals.

quiz.q: A saturated solution is one that:
opts: Cannot dissolve more solute at a given temperature
quiz.explanation: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a given temperature. No more solute can dissolve.

quiz.q: Why is minimum amount of hot solvent used in crystallization?
opts: To obtain maximum yield of crystals
quiz.explanation: Using minimum hot solvent ensures the solution is saturated, so maximum amount of crystals form when the solution cools.
